does: This paper investigates the limitations of current machine unlearning evaluation metrics for large language models by demonstrating that standard success measures—which test performance degradation on specific target datasets—fail to capture whether models have actually forgotten underlying knowledge. The authors show that LLMs can pass conventional unlearning benchmarks while retaining strong capabilities on semantically related content adjacent to the target data. They propose Proximal Surrogate Generation (PSG), an automated stress-testing framework that generates content semantically similar to the unlearning target to reveal whether apparent forgetting masks retained generalizations. Their core finding exposes the "erasure illusion": the gap between successfully removing verbatim content and truly eliminating the broader knowledge and behaviors derived from that content.
connects: This work builds on machine unlearning research that has grown increasingly important for copyright compliance and AI safety, extending beyond earlier benchmarks focused on exact dataset removal. It responds to prior evaluation paradigms that measured unlearning success through degraded performance on target datasets, similar to methods used in membership inference and model extraction literature. The paper connects to broader work on knowledge retention and semantic understanding in language models, drawing on insights from probing tasks and behavioral evaluation methods. It also relates to debates in mechanistic interpretability about whether removing training data truly eliminates underlying capabilities, and to safety research examining whether model behaviors persist despite apparent mitigations.
enables: This work establishes stress-testing as a critical evaluation methodology for machine unlearning, suggesting future research should focus on more comprehensive behavioral verification beyond dataset-level metrics. The PSG framework opens avenues for developing better unlearning algorithms that target underlying knowledge rather than surface-level memorization, potentially improving compliance with copyright and data removal regulations. It enables investigation of what constitutes true knowledge removal in neural networks and could inform more robust safety interventions. The findings motivate development of unlearning techniques that verify complete knowledge erasure through semantic coverage, informing future AI systems where documented removal of training influences is legally or ethically required.
